title: module.v1 — the shape of a module manifest
description: A module is a unit of engine capability. Its files are scattered across the repository by surface (its checks under .engine/check/, its commands under .claude/, and so on), so the manifest is the one place that records what belongs to the module — which makes a clean uninstall, the consistency checks, and the engine self-map possible. Each module owns exactly one manifest at .engine/modules/<id>/manifest.json. This schema governs the manifest's shape — its fields and no others. It does not decide whether the module's dependencies are present or whether its declared files actually exist; that is the module-consistency check's job.

id.description: The module's stable identifier, matching the folder it lives in (.engine/modules/<id>/). Lower-case letters, digits, and hyphens; it stays fixed for the life of the module so its history can be tracked.

version.description: The module's version, as a dotted number (for example 1.4.0), optionally with a pre-release suffix such as -dev during development. This is internal bookkeeping used to drive upgrades and migrations; an operator sees a single engine version, not per-module numbers.

status.description: How the module is carried: required (a foundation, always present), default-on (installed unless the operator opts out), optional (installed only if chosen), experimental (opt-in, not yet stable), or retired (kept for migration history, no longer active).

provides.description: The files this module owns, listed file-precisely (exact paths or globs) and grouped by category — usually a surface name such as check, schema, or tool, plus a foundation group for engine-config files that are not a surface. The groups together must not overlap with another module's files: every engine file is owned by exactly one module (or is a named foundation artifact). This is what makes uninstall and the self-map exact.

depends.description: Other modules this one needs, each named with an optional version range (for example { "core": ">=1.0.0" }). An empty range means any version. This is a presence-and-range assertion the consistency check verifies — that the named module is installed and in range — not a problem solved by a version resolver.

wires.description: The shared-state edits this module makes beyond copying its files, each drawn from a fixed, reversible vocabulary so every edit can be cleanly undone on uninstall. Each entry names its kind and carries that kind's required fields, enforced per-seam below now that the wiring library has landed. The five kinds are the only ones allowed — there is no free-form escape hatch, because an un-reversible edit to shared state is exactly the failure the module system exists to prevent.

type.description: Which shared seam this edit touches.

event.description: The lifecycle event this hook fires on (for example PreToolUse), as named by the hooks system.

matcher.description: Which tools or contexts the hook applies to, as a matcher string.

hook.description: The hook entry itself — its command (which must point into .engine/) and its type; the wiring library validates the command when it applies the wire.

name.description: The engine-prefixed server name (engine-...), the key under which the definition is registered in the root .mcp.json.

definition.description: The MCP server definition (its command and args) written into .mcp.json under the server name.

name.description: The surface name this record introduces in the engine ontology catalog.

record.description: The catalog record for the surface, written into the engine-owned ontology catalog.

value.description: The permission string added to the operator's allow-list (a bare string the engine never auto-removes).

key.description: The engine-managed block id (the fence key) under which the lines are added to the root .gitignore.

lines.description: The .gitignore lines the engine manages inside its comment-fenced block.

migrations.description: Version-keyed data or schema transforms, run in dependency order when the engine is upgraded. Each key is the module version the transform brings the module TO (for example 1.4.0); its value names the transform to run at that version. Empty or absent for a module that needs none. A 'data' transform reshapes a gitignored store and is backed up before it runs, so the change is reversible; a 'config' transform reshapes a committed file, which a reverted upgrade restores on its own.

description.description: One plain-language line describing what this transform reshapes and why. It is shown to the operator in the upgrade's pull-request description, so it must read in plain language without engine jargon.

run.description: The transform code to run, as a path relative to the module's own directory (.engine/modules/<id>/) pointing at a Python file that exposes a migrate(context) function. The file must be one of the module's provided files, so it is installed and owned like the rest of the module.

kind.description: Which kind of state the transform reshapes: 'data' for a gitignored store (snapshotted before it runs so the change is reversible), or 'config' for a committed file (restored on its own when an upgrade is reverted).
